mnemonics: K-Means Kids: Keep Moving Neurons 'til Centers Stabilize (like kids finding their group in a game of tag)
keyPoints: The k-Means algorithm can be implemented as a simple neural network with no hidden layers.
The network uses competitive learning, where only the winning output neuron (the one closest to the input) has its weights updated.
Weight updates move the neuron's weight vector closer to the current input data point, effectively making it a better representative (center) for that cluster.
commonMistakes: Confusing it with a standard Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP); it has no hidden layers and uses a fundamentally different learning rule.
Forgetting to normalize the data, which can prevent the Euclidean distance from being a meaningful measure for the competitive layer.
Assuming the network will always find the global optimum; it is highly sensitive to the initial random placement of the centroids (weights) and can converge to a local minimum.
